ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Mee Tai Bak or silver needle noodle dishes; broader 'noodle dish' class used as the closest defensible anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of fried mee tai bak with Chinese sausage weighs approximately 450 g, representing a typical single-person hawker or casual dining serving in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (HPB Foodo / Singapore Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB entry exists for 'Fried Mee Tai Bak with Chinese Sausage' as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entries for stir-fried rice noodle dishes, Chinese sausage (lap cheong), egg, and pork lard components. Input values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against HPB component data and retained where consistent. Energy cross-check: 6.22g protein × 4 + 12.22g fat × 9 + 8.89g carbohydrate × 4 = 24.88 + 109.98 + 35.56 = 170.42 kcal ≈ 173.33 kcal (within rounding tolerance). Values accepted.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; Rice noodles (white, boiled) GI reference; University of Sydney GI Database — rice noodle analogous entry
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried mee tai bak with Chinese sausage. Mee tai bak (silver needle noodles) are made from rice flour and water, closely analogous to white rice noodles. The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI of approximately 53 (low) for white rice noodles (boiled). Stir-frying with fat (pork lard), protein (egg, Chinese sausage), and dark soy sauce lowers the effective glycaemic response relative to plain boiled noodles. GI of 53 is used as a conservative analogous estimate. GL per serving = (53 × 8.89 × 4.50) / 100 = (53 × 40.005) / 100 = 2120.265 / 100 ≈ 21.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (400 mg/100 g) from Chinese sausage, dark soy sauce, and pork lard, with low potassium and fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its 400 mg sodium per 100 g (1800 mg per standard 450 g plate); if consumed, request reduced soy sauce, omit or halve the lap cheong, and substitute pork lard with a small amount of vegetable oil.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (8.89 g/100 g; ~40 g per plate) from refined rice noodles with low dietary fibre (0.67 g/100 g) and medium-low GI (~53)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes may consume this dish in controlled portions (half-plate, ~225 g) as the co-ingested fat and protein moderate the glycaemic response, but should pair it with non-starchy vegetables to improve fibre intake and limit lap cheong to reduce added sugar and saturated fat.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (4.44 g/100 g) and cholesterol (62.22 mg/100 g) from pork lard and Chinese sausage, combined with high sodium (400 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish sparingly; the combination of pork lard (saturated fat), lap cheong (processed red meat, sodium), and dark soy sauce creates a high cardiovascular risk profile — substitute lard with canola oil, reduce soy sauce, and limit portion to no more than 150 g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory processed red meat (lap cheong) and saturated fat (pork lard) with no meaningful omega-3 or anti-inflammatory components
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise this dish due to the pro-inflammatory combination of processed cured pork (lap cheong) and pork lard; replace lap cheong with tofu or chicken and substitute lard with extra-virgin olive oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Mee Tai Bak with Chinese Sausage (炒米台目配腊肠) is a popular Singaporean hawker stir-fry dish featuring mee tai bak — short, stubby, pin-shaped rice noodles also known as silver needle noodles or rat noodles — cooked in a wok over high heat with sliced lap cheong (Chinese dried pork sausage), egg, dark soy sauce, and rendered pork lard. The dish is a staple at Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops (kopitiams), typically served as a single-plate meal for breakfast, lunch, or dinner. Mee tai bak has a smooth, slightly chewy texture and a mild rice flavour that absorbs the rich, savoury-sweet notes of dark soy sauce and the aromatic fat of pork lard. Lap cheong contributes a distinctive sweet-savoury cured pork flavour and a firm, slightly waxy bite. The egg is scrambled or folded in during stir-frying, adding richness and protein. Crispy pork lard bits (zhū yóu zhā) are often scattered on top as a garnish, providing a crunchy textural contrast. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 173 kcal, 6.2 g protein, 12.2 g fat (4.4 g saturated), 8.9 g carbohydrate, 1.1 g sugar, 0.67 g dietary fibre, and 400 mg sodium. A standard 450 g plate delivers approximately 780 kcal and 1800 mg sodium. The dish is not suitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its high sodium and saturated fat content from lap cheong and pork lard. For type 2 diabetes management, portion control is essential given the refined rice noodle base and low-medium glycaemic index (~53). The dish is not halal due to pork-derived ingredients.

Input values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against HPB component data and are internally consistent (energy cross-check within rounding tolerance). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using white rice noodles as the closest approved reference. Confidence is therefore medium.

GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on white rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for mee tai bak with Chinese sausage was found in approved sources. GI 53 is correctly categorized as 'low' (GI ≤ 55 per University of Sydney GI Database guidelines). The dish contains pork-derived ingredients (lap cheong, pork lard) and is not halal or kosher.
